party_a.current_emotional_state: cognitively engaged but emotionally defended — maintains systemic framing across multiple turns without revealing personal stakes or vulnerability

emotional_arc.assessment: cognitively engaged but emotionally guarded — presenting a complex problem frame in logistical terms without revealing emotional stakes or vulnerability
cognitively engaged but emotionally defended — maintains systemic framing across multiple turns without revealing personal stakes or vulnerability

accumulated_interests.interest: predictability and control over multiple moving parts
avoiding piecemeal decisions that might leave them exposed or disadvantaged
maintaining some degree of coordination authority across domains
preventing Party B from making unilateral changes in any domain that ripple into others
predictability and advance notice across all domains
protection against unilateral changes by Party B that create cascading disruption
maintaining coordination authority or veto capacity over decisions
avoiding vulnerability that comes from piecemeal resolution

risk_signal_history.signal: potential emotional avoidance — complex systemic framing may be deflecting from underlying relational or emotional concerns
possible anxiety about loss of control if issues are unbundled
emotional avoidance — complex framing may be defensive barrier to naming relational or emotional needs
possible control anxiety masked as systems thinking

relational_posture_progression.posture: assertive agenda-setter — defined problem structure, scope, and interdependencies without checking whether Party B shares this frame
dominant framer — has defined problem scope, interdependencies, and resolution structure across two turns without inviting or acknowledging Party B's independent frame

scaffold_divergence_log.model_value: undetermined — Party A has set the frame but Party B has not responded; risk of default power imbalance if Party B does not get equal airtime and problem-definition authority

unilateral framing with deferential compliance — Party A controls problem definition and Party B validates without contributing independent content. This is not cooperative engagement; it is structurally imbalanced participation. After three turns, this pattern is diagnostically significant and requires intervention.

notes: Turn 1 represents session initiation — no party input has been received yet
Scaffold perception indicates apparent stability but reflects absence of data rather than positive signal
No psychological state can be assessed because parties have not yet engaged substantively
No interests can be inferred because no positions or concerns have been articulated
Party A's systemic framing ('all four issues must be solved together') is a strong cognitive organizing structure that may reflect genuine interdependency or may reflect anxiety about unbundling
The absence of emotional language from Party A is notable — no mention of children's experience, relational tension, or their own stress
Critical gap: Party B has been completely silent, creating risk that Solomon's response to Party A's frame could inadvertently validate it before Party B has had opportunity to contest or refine it
Party B's three-turn absence of independent content is the dominant feature of this session state
This is not reactive agreement (where Party B would say 'yes, and I'm also concerned about X'). This is content-free validation.
The domain reasoner and perception agent both flag this pattern as blocking. I concur.

Before any option work can be responsible, Party B must articulate at least one independent interest, priority, or concern — even if it aligns with Party A's frame, it must be stated in their own words from their own experience.

party_b.current_emotional_state: deferential compliance posture — now third speaking turn with no independent content, only agreement with Party A's frame

emotional_arc.assessment: deferential compliance posture — now third speaking turn with no independent content, only agreement with Party A's frame

accumulated_interests.interest: NONE CONFIDENTLY INFERRED — Party B has not articulated independent priorities, concerns, or needs across three opportunities

risk_signal_history.signal: prolonged silence — Party B has had no opportunity to speak, creating risk of mediator-Party A alignment if not corrected immediately
CRITICAL: participation incapacity pattern — three turns without independent content suggests possible power imbalance, intimidation, or internal barrier to authentic participation
default agreement pattern may indicate Party B believes their interests don't matter or fear consequences of disagreement
absence of emotional content across three turns is atypical for separation mediation and suggests guarded or suppressed participation
